basmala: In the Name of God, the Infinitely Merciful, the Most Merciful.
intro: Quraysh — named after the Prophet's tribe — is a brief Meccan sūra closely linked to the previous one, al-Fīl (sūra 105, “The Elephant”), which recalls how God turned away Abraha's army that had come to destroy the Kaʿba. Some traditions report that in Ubayy ibn Kaʿb's codex sūras 105 and 106 were one, and that ʿUmar ibn al-Khaṭṭāb would recite them together in prayer. The first two verses recall a historical grace: the security the Quraysh enjoyed for their caravans — in winter toward Yemen, in summer toward Syria — a security granted by the great neighbouring powers because of their role as guardians of the Kaʿba. The two following verses draw the consequence: let them therefore worship the Lord of this House, who has fed them and freed them from fear. The sūra condenses in four lines the Quranic logic of gift: to acknowledge the grace received, and to turn the heart toward the One who gave it.

versets.trad: For the secure passage of the Quraysh,
versets.commentaire: īlāf (إِيلَاف) comes from the root ʾ-l-f, which first names familiarity, habit — that to which one is bound by heart, that which one is used to. The word came to designate the pacts of security granted to the Quraysh by the great neighbours — Byzantium, Persia, Abyssinia, Yemen — so that they might travel without fear. The verse opens with the causal preposition li-, “for, by reason of”: it is by reason of this secured bond that the Quraysh are called, in verse 3, to worship.

mots.def: preposition: for, by reason of; introduces the motive.
īlāf: the bond of habit, familiarity; here the union of the Quraysh around their charge and the pacts of security granted to them. From the root ʾ-l-f: to be familiar, accustomed.
Quraysh: name of the Prophet's tribe, guardian of the Kaʿba in Mecca.

versets.trad: their secure passage on the journey of winter and of summer,
versets.commentaire: The Quraysh had two great caravans: in winter toward Yemen, where they procured spices and goods coming from India; in summer toward Syria, where they sold these goods and bought those of the land. The verse recalls the very concrete rhythm of their life. God is not only the Lord of sacred things: He is also Lord of roads, of seasons, of trade — of all that makes a life possible.

mots.def: īlāf + suffix pronoun: their bond; refers back to v. 1 and specifies it.
riḥla: journey, expedition, caravan.
shitāʾ: winter; the season of the caravan toward Yemen.
conjunction: and.
ṣayf: summer; the season of the caravan toward Syria.

versets.trad: so let them worship the Lord of this House,
versets.commentaire: hādhā l-bayt (هَٰذَا البَيْت): “this House” — the Kaʿba, the sanctuary the Quraysh are charged to keep. The verse turns the key of the sūra: it was not their idols nor their commercial cunning that kept them in peace, but the Rabb (رَبّ) of this House — its Lord. The duty follows naturally: let them worship the One to whom they owe their security, their food, and the very life of their tribe.

mots.def: linking particle: so, then.
command particle (lām al-amr): marks the 3rd person imperative, “let him/let them”.
ʿabada: to worship, to serve; jussive imperfect plural, with the command particle: let them worship.
rabb: the Lord, the Master; in the construct accusative: “Lord of”.
masc. sing. near demonstrative: this.
bayt: house; here “the House” par excellence — the Kaʿba.

versets.ar: الَّذِي أَطْعَمَهُم مِّن جُوعٍ وَآمَنَهُم مِّنْ خَوْفٍ
versets.tr: alladhī aṭʿamahum min jūʿin wa-āmanahum min khawf
versets.trad: who fed them in hunger and made them safe from fear.
versets.commentaire: Two fundamental evils close the sūra: jūʿ (جُوع), hunger, and khawf (خَوْف), fear. In the life of a people, all begins there — to eat and to feel safe. To Quraysh, God has given both. The sūra does not flatter the tribe: it binds it. In the Quran a gift is never merely a favour; it calls for acknowledgment. The verse echoes 16:112: “God sets forth a parable: a town secure and at peace, its provision coming unto it abundantly from every side. Yet it was ungrateful for the blessings of God; so God let it taste the garment of hunger and fear for what they had wrought.”

mots.def: masc. sing. relative pronoun: who, the one who.
aṭʿama: to give to eat, to feed; perfect + plural suffix pronoun: He fed them.
mots.gram: perfect verb + suffix pronoun, 3rd pers. masc. sing.

mots.def: preposition: from, out of; here “against hunger”, “from hunger”.
jūʿ: hunger; one of the two fundamental evils of collective life, with fear.
conjunction: and.
āmana: to make safe, to grant security; perfect + plural suffix pronoun.
mots.gram: perfect verb + suffix pronoun, 3rd pers. masc. sing.

mots.def: preposition: from, against.
khawf: fear, dread; the second fundamental evil, which undoes common life as deeply as hunger.
